attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: Caching in Next.js
3	nav_title: Caching
4	description: An overview of caching mechanisms in Next.js.
5	---
6	
7	Next.js improves your application's performance and reduces costs by caching rendering work and data requests. This page provides an in-depth look at Next.js caching mechanisms, the APIs you can use to configure them, and how they interact with each other.
8	
9	> **Good to know**: This page helps you understand how Next.js works under the hood but is **not** essential knowledge to be productive with Next.js. Most of Next.js' caching heuristics are determined by your API usage and have defaults for the best performance with zero or minimal configuration. If you instead want to jump to examples, [start here](/docs/app/getting-started/fetching-data).
10	
11	## Overview
12	
13	Here's a high-level overview of the different caching mechanisms and their purpose:
14	
15	| Mechanism                                   | What                       | Where  | Purpose                                         | Duration                        |
16	| ------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ------ | ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
17	| [Request Memoization](#request-memoization) | Return values of functions | Server | Re-use data in a React Component tree           | Per-request lifecycle           |
18	| [Data Cache](#data-cache)                   | Data                       | Server | Store data across user requests and deployments | Persistent (can be revalidated) |
19	| [Full Route Cache](#full-route-cache)       | HTML and RSC payload       | Server | Reduce rendering cost and improve performance   | Persistent (can be revalidated) |
20	| [Router Cache](#client-side-router-cache)   | RSC Payload                | Client | Reduce server requests on navigation            | User session or time-based      |
21	
22	By default, Next.js will cache as much as possible to improve performance and reduce cost. This means routes are **statically rendered** and data requests are **cached** unless you opt out. The diagram below shows the default caching behavior: when a route is statically rendered at build time and when a static route is first visited.
23	
24	<Image
25	  alt="Diagram showing the default caching behavior in Next.js for the four mechanisms, with HIT, MISS and SET at build time and when a route is first visited."
26	  srcLight="/docs/light/caching-overview.png"
27	  srcDark="/docs/dark/caching-overview.png"
28	  width="1600"
29	  height="1179"
30	/>
31	
32	Caching behavior changes depending on whether the route is statically or dynamically rendered, data is cached or uncached, and whether a request is part of an initial visit or a subsequent navigation. Depending on your use case, you can configure the caching behavior for individual routes and data requests.
33	
34	Fetch caching is **not** supported in `proxy`. Any fetches done inside of your `proxy` will be uncached.
35	
36	## Rendering Strategies
37	
38	To understand how caching works in Next.js, it's helpful to understand the rendering strategies available. The rendering strategy determines when your route's HTML is generated, which directly impacts what can be cached.
39	
40	### Static Rendering
41	
42	With Static Rendering, routes are rendered at **build time** or in the background after [data revalidation](/docs/app/guides/incremental-static-regeneration). The result is cached and can be reused across requests. Static routes are fully cached in the [Full Route Cache](#full-route-cache).
43	
44	### Dynamic Rendering
45	
46	With Dynamic Rendering, routes are rendered at **request time**. This happens when your route uses request-specific information like cookies, headers, or search params.
47	
48	A route becomes dynamic when it uses any of these APIs:
49	
50	- [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies)
51	- [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers)…(+36309)

5	---

Most of Next.js' caching heuristics are determined by your API usage and have defaults for the best performance with zero or minimal configuration. If you instead want to jump to examples, [start here](/docs/app/getting-started/fetching-data).
10	
11	## Overview
12	
13	Here's a high-level overview of the different caching mechanisms and their purpose:
14	
15	| Mechanism                                   | What                       | Where  | Purpose                                         | Duration                        |
16	| ------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ------ | ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
17	| [Request Memoization](#request-memoization) | Return values of functions | Server | Re-use data in a React Component tree           | Per-request lifecycle           |

20	| [Router Cache](#client-side-router-cache)   | RSC Payload                | Client | Reduce server requests on navigation            | User session or time-based      |
21	
22	By default, Next.js will cache as much as possible to improve performance and reduce cost. This means routes are **statically rendered** and data requests are **cached** unless you opt out. The diagram below shows the default caching behavior: when a route is statically rendered at build time and when a static route is first visited.
23	
24	<Image
25	  alt="Diagram showing the default caching behavior in Next.js for the four mechanisms, with HIT, MISS and SET at build time and when a route is first visited."
26	  srcLight="/docs/light/caching-overview.png"

32	Caching behavior changes depending on whether the route is statically or dynamically rendered, data is cached or uncached, and whether a request is part of an initial visit or a subsequent navigation. Depending on your use case, you can configure the caching behavior for individual routes and data requests.

35

41

43	
44	### Dynamic Rendering
45	
46	With Dynamic Rendering, routes are rendered at **request time**. This happens when your route uses request-specific information like cookies, headers, or search params.
47	
48	A route becomes dynamic when it uses any of these APIs:
49	
50	- [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies)
51	- [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers)…(+36309)

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: How to create forms with Server Actions
3	nav_title: Forms
4	description: Learn how to create forms in Next.js with React Server Actions.
5	---
6	
7	React Server Actions are [Server Functions](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-functions) that execute on the server. They can be called in Server and Client Components to handle form submissions. This guide will walk you through how to create forms in Next.js with Server Actions.
8	
9	## How it works
10	
11	React extends the HTML [`<form>`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/form) element to allow Server Actions to be invoked with the [`action`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Elements/form#action) attribute.
12	
13	When used in a form, the function automatically receives the [`FormData`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/FormData/FormData) object. You can then extract the data using the native [`FormData` methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/FormData#instance_methods):
14	
15	```tsx filename="app/invoices/page.tsx" switcher
16	export default function Page() {
17	  async function createInvoice(formData: FormData) {
18	    'use server'
19	
20	    const rawFormData = {
21	      customerId: formData.get('customerId'),
22	      amount: formData.get('amount'),
23	      status: formData.get('status'),
24	    }
25	
26	    // mutate data
27	    // revalidate the cache
28	  }
29	
30	  return <form action={createInvoice}>...</form>
31	}
32	```
33	
34	```jsx filename="app/invoices/page.js" switcher
35	export default function Page() {
36	  async function createInvoice(formData) {
37	    'use server'
38	
39	    const rawFormData = {
40	      customerId: formData.get('customerId'),
41	      amount: formData.get('amount'),
42	      status: formData.get('status'),
43	    }
44	
45	    // mutate data
46	    // revalidate the cache
47	  }
48	
49	  return <form action={createInvoice}>...</form>
50	}
51	```
52	
53	> **Good to know:** When working with forms that have multiple fields, use JavaScript's [`Object.fromEntries()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Object/fromEntries). For example: `const rawFormData = Object.fromEntries(formData)`. Note that this object will contain extra properties prefixed with `$ACTION_`.
54	
55	## Passing additional arguments
56	
57	Outside of form fields, you can pass additional arguments to a Server Function using the JavaScript [`bind`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Function/bind) method. For example, to pass the `userId` argument to the `updateUser` Server Function:
58	
59	```tsx filename="app/client-component.tsx" highlight={6} switcher
60	'use client'
61	
62	import { updateUser } from './actions'
63	
64	export function UserProfile({ userId }: { userId: string }) {
65	  const updateUserWithId = updateUser.bind(null, userId)
66	
67	  return (
68	    <form action={updateUserWithId}>
69	      <input type="text" name="name" />
70	      <button type="submit">Update User Name</button>
71	    </form>
72	  )
73	}
74	```
75	
76	```jsx filename="app/client-component.js" highlight={6} switcher
77	'use client'
78	
79	import { updateUser } from './actions'
80	
81	export function UserProfile({ userId }) {
82	  const updateUserWithId = updateUser.bind(null, userId)
83	
84	  return (
85	    <form action={updateUserWithId}>
86	      <input type="text" name="name" />
87	      <button type="submit">Update User Name</button>
88	    </form>
89	  )
90	}
91	```
92	
93	The Server Function will receive the `userId` as an additional argument:
94	
95	```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher
96	'use server'
97	
98	export async function updateUser(userId: string, formData: FormData) {}
99	```
100	
101	```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher
102	'use server'
103	
104	export async function updateUser(userId, formData) {}
105	```
106	
107	> **Good to know**:
108	>
109	> - An alternative is to pass argument…(+11783)

2	title: How to create forms with Server Actions
3	nav_title: Forms

5	---
6	
7	React Server Actions are [Server Functions](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-functions) that execute on the server. They can be called in Server and Client Components to handle form submissions. This guide will walk you through how to create forms in Next.js with Server Actions.
8	
9	## How it works
10	
11	React extends the HTML [`<form>`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/form) element to allow Server Actions to be invoked with the [`action`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Elements/form#action) attribute.
12	
13	When used in a form, the function automatically receives the [`FormData`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/FormData/FormData) object. You can then extract the data using the native [`FormData` methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/FormData#instance_methods):
14	
15	```tsx filename="app/invoices/page.tsx" switcher
16	export default function Page() {
17	  async function createInvoice(formData: FormData) {
18	    'use server'
19	
20	    const rawFormData = {
21	      customerId: formData.get('customerId'),
22	      amount: formData.get('amount'),
23	      status: formData.get('status'),
24	    }
25	
26	    // mutate data
27	    // revalidate the cache
28	  }
29	
30	  return <form action={createInvoice}>...</form>
31	}
32	```
33	
34	```jsx filename="app/invoices/page.js" switcher
35	export default function Page() {
36	  async function createInvoice(formData) {
37	    'use server'
38	
39	    const rawFormData = {
40	      customerId: formData.get('customerId'),
41	      amount: formData.get('amount'),
42	      status: formData.get('status'),
43	    }
44	
45	    // mutate data
46	    // revalidate the cache
47	  }
48	
49	  return <form action={createInvoice}>...</form>
50	}
51	```
52	
53	> **Good to know:** When working with forms that have multiple fields, use JavaScript's [`Object.fromEntries()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Object/fromEntries). For example: `const rawFormData = Object.fromEntries(formData)`. Note that this object will contain extra properties prefixed with `$ACTION_`.
54	
55	## Passing additional arguments
56	
57	Outside of form fields, you can pass additional arguments to a Server Function using the JavaScript [`bind`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Function/bind) method. For example, to pass the `userId` argument to the `updateUser` Server Function:
58	
59	```tsx filename="app/client-component.tsx" highlight={6} switcher
60	'use client'
61	
62	import { updateUser } from './actions'
63	
64	export function UserProfile({ userId }: { userId: string }) {
65	  const updateUserWithId = updateUser.bind(null, userId)
66	
67	  return (
68	    <form action={updateUserWithId}>
69	      <input type="text" name="name" />
70	      <button type="submit">Update User Name</button>
71	    </form>
72	  )
73	}
74	```
75	
76	```jsx filename="app/client-component.js" highlight={6} switcher
77	'use client'
78	
79	import { updateUser } from './actions'
80	
81	export function UserProfile({ userId }) {
82	  const updateUserWithId = updateUser.bind(null, userId)
83	
84	  return (
85	    <form action={updateUserWithId}>
86	      <input type="text" name="name" />
87	      <button type="submit">Update User Name</button>
88	    </form>
89	  )
90	}
91	```
92	
93	The Server Function will receive the `userId` as an additional argument:
94	
95	```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher
96	'use server'
97	
98	export async function updateUser(userId: string, formData: FormData) {}
99	```
100	
101	```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher
102	'use server'
103	
104	export async function updateUser(userId, formData) {}
105	```
106	
107	> **Good to know**:
108	>
109	> - An alternative is to pass argument…(+11783)

1	---
2	title: Updating Data
3	description: Learn how to mutate data using Server Functions and Server Actions in Next.js.
4	related:
5	  title: API Reference
6	  description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath
9	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag
10	    - app/api-reference/functions/redirect
11	---
12	
13	You can update data in Next.js using React's [Server Functions](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-functions). This page will go through how you can [create](#creating-server-functions) and [invoke](#invoking-server-functions) Server Functions.
14	
15	## What are Server Functions?
16	
17	A **Server Function** is an asynchronous function that runs on the server. They can be called from the client through a network request, which is why they must be asynchronous.
18	
19	In an `action` or mutation context, they are also called **Server Actions**.
20	
21	> **Good to know:** A Server Action is a Server Function used in a specific way (for handling form submissions and mutations). Server Function is the broader term.
22	
23	By convention, a Server Action is an async function used with [`startTransition`](https://react.dev/reference/react/startTransition). This happens automatically when the function is:
24	
25	- Passed to a `<form>` using the `action` prop.
26	- Passed to a `<button>` using the `formAction` prop.
27	
28	In Next.js, Server Actions integrate with the framework's [caching](/docs/app/guides/caching) architecture. When an action is invoked, Next.js can return both the updated UI and new data in a single server roundtrip.
29	
30	Behind the scenes, actions use the `POST` method, and only this HTTP method can invoke them.
31	
32	## Creating Server Functions
33	
34	A Server Function can be defined by using the [`use server`](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-server) directive. You can place the directive at the top of an **asynchronous** function to mark the function as a Server Function, or at the top of a separate file to mark all exports of that file.
35	
36	```ts filename="app/lib/actions.ts" switcher
37	export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
38	  'use server'
39	  const title = formData.get('title')
40	  const content = formData.get('content')
41	
42	  // Update data
43	  // Revalidate cache
44	}
45	
46	export async function deletePost(formData: FormData) {
47	  'use server'
48	  const id = formData.get('id')
49	
50	  // Update data
51	  // Revalidate cache
52	}
53	```
54	
55	```js filename="app/lib/actions.js" switcher
56	export async function createPost(formData) {
57	  'use server'
58	  const title = formData.get('title')
59	  const content = formData.get('content')
60	
61	  // Update data
62	  // Revalidate cache
63	}
64	
65	export async function deletePost(formData) {
66	  'use server'
67	  const id = formData.get('id')
68	
69	  // Update data
70	  // Revalidate cache
71	}
72	```
73	
74	### Server Components
75	
76	Server Functions can be inlined in Server Components by adding the `"use server"` directive to the top of the function body:
77	
78	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
79	export default function Page() {
80	  // Server Action
81	  async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
82	    'use server'
83	    // ...
84	  }
85	
86	  return <></>
87	}
88	```
89	
90	```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher
91	export default function Page() {
92	  // Server Action
93	  async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
94	    'use server'
95	    // ...
96	  }
97	
98	  return <></>
99	}
100	```
101	
102	> **Good to know:** Server Components support progressive enhancement by default, meaning forms that call Server Actions will be submitted even if JavaScript hasn't loaded yet or is disabled.
103	
104	### Client Components
105	
106	It's not possible to define Server Functions in Client Components. However, you can invoke them in Client Components by importing them from a …(+13304)

68

73	
74	### Server Components
75	
76	Server Functions can be inlined in Server Components by adding the `"use server"` directive to the top of the function body:
77	
78	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
79	export default function Page() {
80	  // Server Action
81	  async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
82	    'use server'
83	    // ...
84	  }
85	
86	  return <></>
87	}
88	```
89	
90	```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher
91	export default function Page() {
92	  // Server Action
93	  async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
94	    'use server'
95	    // ...
96	  }
97	
98	  return <></>
99	}
100	```
101	
102	> **Good to know:** Server Components support progressive enhancement by default, meaning forms that call Server Actions will be submitted even if JavaScript hasn't loaded yet or is disabled.
103	
104	### Client Components
105	
106	It's not possible to define Server Functions in Client Components. However, you can invoke them in Client Components by importing them from a …(+13304)

attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: Caching and Revalidating
3	description: Learn how to cache and revalidate data in your application.
4	related:
5	  title: API Reference
6	  description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/functions/fetch
9	    - app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag
10	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag
11	    - app/api-reference/functions/updateTag
12	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath
13	    - app/api-reference/functions/unstable_cache
14	---
15	
16	Caching is a technique for storing the result of data fetching and other computations so that future requests for the same data can be served faster, without doing the work again. While revalidation allows you to update cache entries without having to rebuild your entire application.
17	
18	Next.js provides a few APIs to handle caching and revalidation. This guide will walk you through when and how to use them.
19	
20	- [`fetch`](#fetch)
21	- [`cacheTag`](#cachetag)
22	- [`revalidateTag`](#revalidatetag)
23	- [`updateTag`](#updatetag)
24	- [`revalidatePath`](#revalidatepath)
25	- [`unstable_cache`](#unstable_cache) (Legacy)
26	
27	## `fetch`
28	
29	By default, [`fetch`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) requests are not cached. You can cache individual requests by setting the `cache` option to `'force-cache'`.
30	
31	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
32	export default async function Page() {
33	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
34	}
35	```
36	
37	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
38	export default async function Page() {
39	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
40	}
41	```
42	
43	> **Good to know**: Although `fetch` requests are not cached by default, Next.js will [pre-render](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) routes that have `fetch` requests and cache the HTML. If you want to guarantee a route is [dynamic](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), use the [`connection` API](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection).
44	
45	To revalidate the data returned by a `fetch` request, you can use the `next.revalidate` option.
46	
47	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
48	export default async function Page() {
49	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
50	}
51	```
52	
53	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
54	export default async function Page() {
55	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
56	}
57	```
58	
59	This will revalidate the data after a specified amount of seconds.
60	
61	You can also tag `fetch` requests to enable on-demand cache invalidation:
62	
63	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
64	export async function getUserById(id: string) {
65	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
66	    next: {
67	      tags: ['user'],
68	    },
69	  })
70	}
71	```
72	
73	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
74	export async function getUserById(id) {
75	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
76	    next: {
77	      tags: ['user'],
78	    },
79	  })
80	}
81	```
82	
83	See the [`fetch` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) to learn more.
84	
85	## `cacheTag`
86	
87	[`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) allows you to tag cached data in [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) so it can be revalidated on-demand. Previously, cache tagging was limited to `fetch` requests, and caching other work required the experimental `unstable_cache` API.
88	
89	With Cache Components, you can use the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive to cache any computation, and `cacheTag` to tag it. This works with database queries, file system operations, and other server-side work.
90	
91	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
92	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
93	
94	export async function getProducts() {
95	  'use cache'
96	…(+7992)

Previously, cache tagging was limited to `fetch` requests, and caching other work required the experimental `unstable_cache` API.

93

attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: Cache Components
3	description: Learn how to use Cache Components and combine the benefits of static and dynamic rendering.
4	related:
5	  title: Next Steps
6	  description: Learn more about the config option for Cache Components.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents
9	    - app/api-reference/directives/use-cache
10	    - app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife
11	    - app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag
12	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag
13	    - app/api-reference/functions/updateTag
14	---
15	
16	> **Good to know:** Cache Components is an opt-in feature. Enable it by setting the `cacheComponents` flag to `true` in your Next config file. See [Enabling Cache Components](#enabling-cache-components) for more details.
17	
18	Cache Components lets you mix static, cached, and dynamic content in a single route, giving you the speed of static sites with the flexibility of dynamic rendering.
19	
20	Server-rendered applications typically force a choice between static pages (fast but stale) and dynamic pages (fresh but slow). Moving this work to the client trades server load for larger bundles and slower initial rendering.
21	
22	Cache Components eliminates these tradeoffs by prerendering routes into a **static HTML shell** that's immediately sent to the browser, with dynamic content updating the UI as it becomes ready.
23	
24	<Image
25	  alt="Partially re-rendered Product Page showing static nav and product information, and dynamic cart and recommended products"
26	  srcLight="/learn/light/thinking-in-ppr.png"
27	  srcDark="/learn/dark/thinking-in-ppr.png"
28	  width="1600"
29	  height="632"
30	/>
31	
32	## How rendering works with Cache Components
33	
34	At build time, Next.js renders your route's component tree. As long as components don't access network resources, certain system APIs, or require an incoming request to render, their output is **automatically added to the static shell**. Otherwise, you must choose how to handle them:
35	
36	- Either defer rendering to request time by wrapping components in React's [`<Suspense>`](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense), [showing fallback UI](#defer-rendering-to-request-time) until the content is ready
37	- Or cache the result using the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive to [include it in the static shell](#using-use-cache) (if no request data is needed)
38	
39	Because this happens ahead of time, before a request arrives, we refer to it as prerendering. This generates a static shell consisting of HTML for initial page loads and a serialized [RSC Payload](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components#on-the-server) for client-side navigation, ensuring the browser receives fully rendered content instantly whether users navigate directly to the URL or transition from another page.
40	
41	Next.js requires you to explicitly handle components that can't complete during prerendering. If they aren't wrapped in `<Suspense>` or marked with `use cache`, you'll see an [`Uncached data was accessed outside of <Suspense>`](https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/blocking-route) error during development and build time.
42	
43	> **Good to know**: Caching can be applied at the component or function level, while fallback UI can be defined around any subtree, which means you can compose static, cached, and dynamic content within a single route.
44	
45	<Image
46	  alt="Diagram showing partially rendered page on the client, with loading UI for chunks that are being streamed."
47	  srcLight="/docs/light/server-rendering-with-streaming.png"
48	  srcDark="/docs/dark/server-rendering-with-streaming.png"
49	  width="1600"
50	  height="785"
51	/>
52	
53	This rendering approach is called **Partial Prerendering**, and it's the default behavior with Cache Components. For the rest of this document, we simply refer to it as "prerendering" which can produce a partial or complete output.
54	
55	> **🎥 Watch:** Wh…(+26090)

14	---
15	
16	> **Good to know:** Cache Components is an opt-in feature. Enable it by setting the `cacheComponents` flag to `true` in your Next config file. See [Enabling Cache Components](#enabling-cache-components) for more details.
17	
18	Cache Components lets you mix static, cached, and dynamic content in a single route, giving you the speed of static sites with the flexibility of dynamic rendering.
19	
20	Server-rendered applications typically force a choice between static pages (fast but stale) and dynamic pages (fresh but slow). Moving this work to the client trades server load for larger bundles and slower initial rendering.
21	
22	Cache Components eliminates these tradeoffs by prerendering routes into a **static HTML shell** that's immediately sent to the browser, with dynamic content updating the UI as it becomes ready.
23	
24	<Image
25	  alt="Partially re-rendered Product Page showing static nav and product information, and dynamic cart and recommended products"
26	  srcLight="/learn/light/thinking-in-ppr.png"
27	  srcDark="/learn/dark/thinking-in-ppr.png"
28	  width="1600"
29	  height="632"
30	/>
31	
32	## How rendering works with Cache Components
33	
34	At build time, Next.js renders your route's component tree. As long as components don't access network resources, certain system APIs, or require an incoming request to render, their output is **automatically added to the static shell**. Otherwise, you must choose how to handle them:
35	
36	- Either defer rendering to request time by wrapping components in React's [`<Suspense>`](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense), [showing fallback UI](#defer-rendering-to-request-time) until the content is ready
37	- Or cache the result using the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive to [include it in the static shell](#using-use-cache) (if no request data is needed)
38	
39	Because this happens ahead of time, before a request arrives, we refer to it as prerendering. This generates a static shell consisting of HTML for initial page loads and a serialized [RSC Payload](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components#on-the-server) for client-side navigation, ensuring the browser receives fully rendered content instantly whether users navigate directly to the URL or transition from another page.
40	
41	Next.js requires you to explicitly handle components that can't complete during prerendering. If they aren't wrapped in `<Suspense>` or marked with `use cache`, you'll see an [`Uncached data was accessed outside of <Suspense>`](https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/blocking-route) error during development and build time.
42	
43	> **Good to know**: Caching can be applied at the component or function level, while fallback UI can be defined around any subtree, which means you can compose static, cached, and dynamic content within a single route.
44	
45	<Image
46	  alt="Diagram showing partially rendered page on the client, with loading UI for chunks that are being streamed."
47	  srcLight="/docs/light/server-rendering-with-streaming.png"
48	  srcDark="/docs/dark/server-rendering-with-streaming.png"
49	  width="1600"
50	  height="785"
51	/>
52	
53	This rendering approach is called **Partial Prerendering**, and it's the default behavior with Cache Components. For the rest of this document, we simply refer to it as "prerendering" which can produce a partial or complete output.
54	
55	> **🎥 Watch:** Wh…(+26090)
